entities.description: Christine Robinson's plain-text machine-readable transcription of Speculum Vitae from British Library Additional MS 33995, deposited with the Oxford Text Archive on 13 May 1983.
entities.label: Oxford Text Archive resource 0022
notes: The complete transcription runs from line 1 at fol. 1ra through line 16096 at fol. 96rb.
The legacy purl.ox.ac.uk identifier is retained as historical identification only; it did not provide a working access route when checked on 2026-07-14.
The OTA item's inherited 1400-1499 date describes a broad witness-era catalog field and is not used as the work's composition date.

bibliographic.citation: Christine Mary Robinson, ‘A machine-readable edition of the text of the Speculum Vitae as attested in British Library MS. Additional 33995, with introduction, glossary and an investigation of claims for the common authorship of the Speculum Vitae and the Prick of Conscience’ (PhD thesis, University of Edinburgh, 1987; ERA date issued 1988).
bibliographic.title: A machine-readable edition of the text of the Speculum Vitae as attested in British Library MS. Additional 33995, with introduction, glossary and an investigation of claims for the common authorship of the Speculum Vitae and the Prick of Conscience

dateStatements.notes: The repository date is preserved without replacing the 1987 date printed on the thesis title page.

entities.description: A two-volume thesis containing an introduction, a complete printed edition of Speculum Vitae from Additional MS 33995, a glossary, and authorship studies.
entities.label: Christine Robinson's machine-readable edition and doctoral thesis
notes: Volume 1 contains the manuscript description, editorial preface, and full 16,096-line edition; volume 2 contains the glossary and comparative authorship studies.
The thesis reports no persuasive evidence for common authorship with the Prick of Conscience.

rights.basis: The Edinburgh Research Archive provides public downloads but its current item metadata exposes no item-specific open license or rights statement.
rights.component: Robinson's thesis text, transcription, editorial apparatus, glossary, and studies
notes: Public access is not evidence of permission to republish the thesis PDFs or transcription.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: second half of the fourteenth century

entities.description: A late-fourteenth-century English parchment codex. Speculum Vitae occupies ff. 1ra-96rb and is followed by the Stimulus Conscientie Minor, the Prick of Conscience, and a Tractatus/Bande of Louyng.

holding.notes: No exact item record was recovered from the British Library's current interim Archives and Manuscripts search on 2026-07-14; the shelfmark and holding are independently documented by the thesis and the 1894 British Museum catalogue.

entities.label: British Library Additional MS 33995
notes: The physical witness is distinct from Robinson's transcription and from any future photographs.

A whole-manuscript reproduction request should cover bindings, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every recto and verso through f. 157, edges or color targets where offered, and should separately map Speculum Vitae to ff. 1ra-96rb.

physicalDescription.dimensions: Leaves average approximately 308 × 200 mm; written frame approximately 245 × 148 mm
notes: The Speculum Vitae scribe writes a late-fourteenth-century anglicana hand, normally about 40-45 lines per column; Robinson localizes the scribal language to northwest Yorkshire.
Latin headings are framed in red, initials have red penwork, and the text includes Latin running titles and marginalia, pointing hands, grotesques, and a terminal Scutum Fidei Trinitatis.
The codex may have belonged to the English Recollects at Douai, later belonged to William Horatio Crawford at Lakelands, Cork, and was bought at the Crawford sale on 18 March 1894.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment; the final leaf is a reused thirteenth-century Latin legal flyleaf

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval composition and surviving fourteenth-century witness predate modern copyright terms by centuries.
rights.component: The underlying fourteenth-century Speculum Vitae text attested by the manuscript, excluding photographs, catalog data, modern transcriptions, editions, and annotations
notes: This does not place modern manuscript photographs, transcriptions, editorial markup, or repository delivery files in the public domain.

reviewNotes: The approved recent_changes XML and the legacy CME_from_OTA XML have semantically identical recovered EEBO text subtrees; their material divergence is the wrapper and repaired HEADER. Both fixities are recorded, but only the approved repaired-header file is modeled as the repository artifact.

The 1983 OTA deposit and the 1987 thesis are kept chronologically distinct. The thesis is a later scholarly realization of Robinson's transcription project, not an intermediary between the witness and OTA.
Rights and access statements are scoped to named components. Public access to a transcription or thesis does not imply permission to redistribute future British Library manuscript photographs.
The absence of a public facsimile is a dated search result as of 2026-07-14, not proof that no unindexed or private surrogate exists.

lineage.summary: The publication artifact is the repaired-header XML at CME/source/recent_changes/additions_2023/CME90022.xml. Its medieval text descends through the University of Michigan/Middle English Compendium's 2023 structured representation from Oxford Text Archive deposit 0022, Christine Robinson's 1983 machine-readable transcription of British Library Additional MS 33995, ff. 1ra-96rb. Robinson's later 1987 doctoral thesis prints and studies the same full transcription but cannot be the source of the earlier OTA deposit. Ralph Hanna's 2008 reading edition was consulted by the 2023 encoders for lineation and divisions rather than used as the base text. No complete public manuscript facsimile was located; the British Library offers manuscript photography by request.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous; formerly attributed to William of Nassyngton
catalogSummary.date: Third quarter of the fourteenth century (approximately 1350-1375); represented by a late-fourteenth-century Additional MS 33995 witness
catalogSummary.region: Yorkshire composition; the represented manuscript's first scribe is localized more narrowly to northwest Yorkshire, a witness-level dialect claim
catalogSummary.title: Speculum Vitae

catalogingSubject.label: Speculum Vitae
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English Speculum Vitae: a 16,096-line catechetical poem in four-stress rhyming couplets, selectively translating and rearranging Laurent d'Orléans's Somme le roi and using the Latin Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice. It excludes British Library Additional MS 33995 as a physical codex, Christine Robinson's transcription and thesis, the 1983 OTA deposit, U-M's 2023 structured encoding, Ralph Hanna's 2008 reading edition, and the General Corpus repository artifact. The historical attribution to William of Nassyngton is retained as disputed attribution history rather than authorship.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Invocation and statement of the work's didactic purpose, including the self-description Mirour of Lyf.

contentParts.label: Pater Noster
contentParts.summary: Introduction to the Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions.

contentParts.label: First words of the Pater Noster
contentParts.summary: Exposition of Pater, Noster, Qui es, and In celis, incorporating the Trinity, Decalogue, Creed, sacraments, and seven virtues.

contentParts.label: Petitions of the Pater Noster and the Gifts
contentParts.summary: The seven petitions followed by the septenary scheme: the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit coordinated with sins, virtues, beatitudes, and moral instruction.

contentParts.label: Conclusion
contentParts.summary: Closing exhortation and prayer for the unnamed translator and audience, ending with a prayer for heavenly bliss through Christ's crucifixion.

assertion.notes: The broad third-quarter placement remains a useful scholarly consensus, but Glover's 2024 reassessment questions a Rolle-dependent terminus post quem used in Hanna's dating argument.

dateStatements.display: Third quarter of the fourteenth century (approximately 1350-1375)
notes: The late-fourteenth-century production date of Additional MS 33995 is witness evidence, not a substitute for composition dating.
The OTA item's 1400-1499 field is not used as the work date.

extent.description: The approved XML represents one complete verse work in one DIV1 with five major DIV2 units, twenty DIV3 units, twenty DIV4 units, 65 HEAD elements, 16,096 L elements, 382 folio-column MILESTONE elements, four P elements, 43 Q elements, 989 HI elements, and eight CLOSER elements. The five major units contain 114, 128, 2,078, 13,696, and 80 lines respectively. These are representation metrics for the approved Additional MS 33995 transcription, not a claim that every manuscript or critical edition shares the same hierarchy or lineation.

formStatements.description: A 16,096-line poem in rhyming couplets, normally four-stress lines. The approved XML encodes individual L elements directly under divisions rather than grouping them in LG elements.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is embedded within a primarily Middle English work; it does not make the poem mixed prose and verse.

scope.description: Lord's Prayer words, biblical and catechetical quotations, rubrics, headings, and other embedded Latin passages

notes: The work is anonymous. William of Nassyngton is retained only as a disputed attributed author so discovery interfaces can explain, rather than repeat, the historical claim.
Yorkshire composition and northwest-Yorkshire scribal dialect are intentionally separate claims: the latter belongs to the represented witness's first scribe.
The 2023 CME hierarchy is a navigation and representation layer influenced by Hanna's reading edition; it is not presumed to reproduce the manuscript's visual hierarchy exactly.

The approved XML's 16,096 lines and five-part structure describe the Additional MS 33995 transcription. Newhauser reports forty-five surviving copies, while DIMEV v1.0 contains forty-seven witness elements and two ghosts; the differing census conventions remain unresolved, and abstract-work variation must not be collapsed into this one witness.

The repository's ignored source cache contains the complete OTA text and header, both complete Robinson thesis volumes, the complete 1894 British Museum catalogue, the complete DIMEV v1.0 data release, the four-page Hanna front-matter preview, and Glover's complete 38-page accepted manuscript. No public full manuscript facsimile was found as of 2026-07-14.

next_steps: Review Glover's complete 2024 argument together with Hanna's dating discussion and all independently dated early witnesses.
Separate source-based termini, manuscript-attestation termini, dialect evidence, and broad literary-historical dating.
Retain the third-quarter-fourteenth-century range at medium confidence until a replacement bound is grounded.
openQuestions.question: How should Speculum Vitae's lower composition bound be revised if direct dependence on Richard Rolle's Form of Living is no longer accepted?

next_steps: Consult Hanna's complete source study and subsequent scholarship, not only the public front-matter preview and review.
Collate distinctive passages against candidate French and Latin recensions while distinguishing source works from surviving physical exemplars.
Reassess proposed Rolle parallels in light of Glover 2024.
openQuestions.question: Which recensions or physical exemplars of Somme le roi and the Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice underlie Speculum Vitae, and what other source dependencies remain secure?

next_steps: Compare Hanna's complete manuscript census with every DIMEV v1.0 witness and ghost entry.
Distinguish complete copies, substantial fragments, isolated excerpts, duplicate descriptions, lost or ghost witnesses, and newly identified material.
Report census counts with the source's counting rule rather than presenting forty-five or forty-seven as universally interchangeable.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 contain forty-seven witness elements and two ghost references for record 423 while Newhauser describes Speculum Vitae as extant in forty-five copies?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether a complete preservation surrogate, legacy microfilm, or prior color image set exists.
If commissioning photography, request the whole codex and map the poem to ff. 1ra-96rb rather than requesting only cropped work pages.
Obtain separate written authorization or restrictions for private research, public Git distribution, scholarly publication, and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of Additional MS 33995 be obtained and mapped to Speculum Vitae with written reuse terms?

next_steps: Collate OTA plain text against the thesis edition after controlling for PDF OCR and page layout.
Audit every italic span and silent expansion category against Robinson's conventions and, when available, manuscript images.
Document U-M-only hierarchy, lineation alignment, and selected manuscript headings separately from Robinson's transcription choices.
openQuestions.question: What textual and editorial differences separate the 1983 OTA deposit, the 1987 thesis edition, and U-M's 2023 structured representation?

assertion.notes: Yorkshire is supported at regional scale; a more exact composition locality has not been established.
This is witness-level copying evidence and is not promoted to an exact authorial origin.

scope.description: Language of the first scribe in the represented Additional MS 33995 copy

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English poet-translator of Speculum Vitae

assertion.notes: The represented witness's conclusion asks prayer for the unnamed translator; it does not name William of Nassyngton.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English poet-translator of Speculum Vitae

assertion.notes: Translation is not mechanically line-for-line: the poet selects, rearranges, expands, and combines source material.

scope.description: Selective Middle English verse translation from Laurent d'Orléans's French Somme le roi, supplemented by Latin material

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English poet-translator of Speculum Vitae

scope.description: Reorganization of Somme le roi around the Lord's Prayer and integration of Latin catechetical material

assertion.notes: The historical attribution existed, but Hanna's analysis as summarized by Newhauser rejects it because only two non-central manuscripts carry the naming interpolation and the alleged John Waldeby source is not the poem's source.

notes: Do not display William of Nassyngton as an accepted author.

subjects.label: The Lord's Prayer and its seven petitions
notes: The Lord's Prayer supplies the organizing framework for the work rather than merely one local topic.

subjects.label: Christian doctrine and the Ten Commandments
The seven gifts of the Holy Spirit

summaries.value: Speculum Vitae is an anonymous Middle English catechetical poem of 16,096 four-stress lines in rhyming couplets. It organizes Christian teaching through the words and seven petitions of the Lord's Prayer, treating the Trinity, Decalogue, Creed, sacraments, virtues and vices, seven gifts of the Holy Spirit, beatitudes, and related moral instruction. Composed in Yorkshire in the third quarter of the fourteenth century, it selectively translates and rearranges Laurent d'Orléans's Somme le roi and incorporates material from the Latin Tabula de utilitate oracionis dominice. The represented Additional MS 33995 copy names no author; the former attribution to William of Nassyngton rests on an interpolation in other manuscripts and is rejected by modern scholarship.

After a 114-line prologue, the poem introduces the Pater Noster and its seven petitions, expounds the prayer's opening words through core catechetical systems, interprets each petition, and then coordinates the seven gifts with opposing sins and corresponding virtues before an 80-line conclusion.

notes: The conventional Latin title is used for discovery; the poem calls itself a Mirour of Lyf at lines 95-96 in the represented witness.
Lines 95-96 say the lesson may be a Mirour of Lyf in which readers can see their whole life.

scope.description: Middle English self-reference in the represented witness

notes: This source title preserves an obsolete William of Nassyngton attribution and an imprecise description of the source language. The main source work is Laurent d'Orléans's French Somme le roi, supplemented by Latin material.

scope.description: Inherited bibliographic title in the 2023 CME header

titles.value: Mirror of life (Middle English version translated from the Latin of the Speculum Vitae attributed to William of Nassington)

scope.description: Opening two lines of the Additional MS 33995 version represented by CME90022

titles.value: Almyghty Godde in Trynyte / In wham anely er persons thre

scope.description: Final two lines of the Additional MS 33995 version represented by CME90022

titles.value: To whilk blisse he vs alle brynge / Yat on ye croyce for vs wald hynge

work.author: Anonymous; formerly attributed to William of Nassyngton
work.dateDisplay: Third quarter of the fourteenth century (approximately 1350-1375); represented by a late-fourteenth-century Additional MS 33995 witness
